params.text:
Professional curiosity

Serious case reviews repeatedly find that warning signs were present but were explained away. The mindset that keeps children safe is professional curiosity – actively looking, listening and questioning rather than taking things at face value.

  • Notice what does not add up, and gently explore it rather than accepting the first explanation.
  • Hold the possibility that “it could happen here” – abuse occurs in every kind of family and community, including ones that seem entirely respectable.
  • Avoid the trap of “disguised compliance,” where a family appears to co-operate just enough to reduce professional concern.
  • Keep the child in focus. Whose voice is being heard, and whose is missing?

params.alt: Illustration for It could happen here

params.text:
Putting it into practice

You do not need to have all the answers. Your job is to notice, to be curious, to record what you see, and to raise concerns without delay. A single piece of information from you may be the missing part of a picture that only makes sense when several people share what they know.

If something does not feel right about a child's welfare, trust that instinct and speak to your Designated Safeguarding Lead. Doing nothing is never the safe option.

params.question: Professional curiosity means accepting a family's explanation so long as it sounds plausible.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Professional curiosity means not simply accepting a plausible-sounding explanation. It means being curious and exploring what does not add up.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Professional curiosity is the opposite – it means looking, questioning and testing explanations rather than taking them at face value.
